ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper offers a clear and useful new perspective on evaluation—advocating measuring data efficiency via simple supervised models across varying training sizes—and provides empirical findings (e.g., non-linear recoverability of similarity/analogy, task-dependent embedding rankings, GloVe100 vs GloVe300 differences) that are informative. However, the technical approach largely applies existing supervised models (RF, SVR, NN, logistic, SVM, CNN) to established datasets and baselines (SimLex999, MEN, WordRep, SST, POS, etc.), so the contribution is primarily a methodological reframing and empirical study rather than a novel algorithmic or theoretical advance. Overall this is a moderate-novelty, incremental but valuable contribution to evaluation practice.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important, practical problem—how to evaluate word embeddings—by proposing a concrete methodology (varying dataset size and simple supervised models, linear and non-linear) and demonstrating new insights (e.g., GloVe100 vs GloVe300 differences and non-linear encoding of similarity/analogy). These findings and the available code are likely to change evaluation practices and be adopted by researchers comparing embeddings across tasks and data-efficiency concerns. While not wholly transformative, the work opens clear follow-on directions (task-dependent rankings, focus on transfer/data-efficiency) and has applicability beyond a narrow niche of cosine-based benchmarks.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-organized (clear intro → methodology → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (focus on data efficiency and supervised/simple tasks). Notation and experimental details appear consistent and well-documented (hyperparameters reported, code available), and writing quality is rated good with adequate related-work coverage. Minor presentation issues remain around communicating computational limitations (e.g., expensive neural regression and RankSVM) and dataset splitting choices, which could be explained slightly more clearly.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments are broad and well-structured: multiple pretrained embeddings (GloVe, PDC, HDC, morph, NMT, skip-gram), diverse tasks (similarity, analogy, sentiment, POS, WordNet membership), and evaluations across varying training set sizes with both linear and non-linear supervised models, plus constant baselines. Sensitivity analyses are present (data-efficiency curves, linear vs non-linear models) and hyperparameters and code are provided, aiding reproducibility. The authors acknowledge and discuss limitations and negative results (MSR/Google analogy overfitting, computational cost of regression NN and RankSVM), but some computational constraints prevented exhaustive methods on analogy which leaves a minor gap. Related work coverage is reported as adequate.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This is squarely within top NLP/ML venue scope: it addresses evaluation of word embeddings, a core NLP/ML concern, and targets researchers/practitioners who build or use embeddings. The methodology and evaluation meet community standards—using many standard datasets (SimLex999, MEN, WordSimilarity353, POS, SST, WordRep analogies), a range of supervised and unsupervised baselines (cosine, 3COSADD/3COSMUL, SVM/RF/CNN/etc.), multiple pretrained embeddings (GloVe, skip-gram, NMT, PDC/HDC), reported hyperparameters, and released code. The problem formulation (data-efficiency and simple supervised tasks) is appropriate and the paper is well positioned within the venue’s research community.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against a wide and appropriate set of baselines (cosine, 3COSADD/3COSMUL, various supervised models including RF, SVR-RBF, SVM, CNN, k-NN, and RankSVM trials) and a broad set of pretrained embeddings (GloVe variants, HPCA, PDC, HDC, morph, NMT, skip-gram). Comparisons appear fair: experiments use the same datasets and metrics, report hyperparameters, make dataset-split choices to avoid overlap (e.g., WordRep for analogies), and provide code for reproducibility. The authors acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably or do not outperform (e.g., RankSVM and regression NN issues) and analyze differences such as GloVe100 vs GloVe300 and nonlinear recoverability of similarity/analogy information.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by a broad experimental evaluation: they test multiple pretrained embeddings (GloVe, PDC, HDC, skip-gram, NMT, morph), use many tasks/datasets (SimLex999, MEN, WS353, RareWords, WordRep analogies, SST, News20, POS, WordNet, etc.), and compare unsupervised cosine baselines and standard supervised models (linear and non-linear) while varying training set size to assess data efficiency. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the empirical methodology appears sound—appropriate baselines, disjoint analogy splits (WordRep) to avoid leakage, reported hyperparameters and available code—though the summary does not report formal statistical significance testing. The conclusions (task-dependent rankings, non-linear encodings, recommendation to evaluate data efficiency with simple supervised tasks) follow from the presented experiments; minor issues are computational constraints on some methods (regression NN, RankSVM) and the lack of explicit statistical tests, which do not undermine the main claims.

main_claims: Evaluation of word embeddings should focus on data efficiency and simple supervised tasks rather than purely unsupervised, cosine-based benchmarks.

Proposed evaluation methodology (varying dataset size and using supervised models, linear and non-linear) reveals differences between embeddings along data efficiency and non-linearity of downstream models, producing new insights (e.g., differences between GloVe100 and GloVe300).
Information about similarity and analogy is often non-linearly encoded in embeddings, so unsupervised cosine-based evaluation can miss recoverable information.
There are no universally good embeddings; embedding rankings are task dependent and one should specify evaluation focus (e.g., transfer/data-efficiency).
_summary.methodology: Evaluate pretrained word embeddings by measuring performance of simple supervised models (both linear and non-linear) across a variety of tasks while varying the amount of training data to assess data efficiency and model non-linearity; include fallback simpler models and constant baseline methods for robustness.
datasets: Google News pretrained skip-gram (used in some experiments)
baselines: Support Vector Regression (SVR) with RBF kernel
Support Vector Machine (SVM) with RBF kernel
Pretrained embeddings compared: GloVe (100,300), HPCA, PDC (100,300), HDC (300), morph, NMT embeddings, skip-gram
metrics: mean rank / ranking change (mean absolute change ~0.6, std 0.2)
limitations: MSR and Google analogy datasets led to overfitting when train and test sets share words, so WordRep was used with disjoint splits.
The regression neural network approach for analogy prediction is very computationally intensive.
RankSVM was computationally intensive and did not outperform other models.
